name: Journal of Emerging Investigators (JEI)
ageNote: Grades 6-12, age 13+ — explicitly welcomes middle schoolers; THE legit MS science option.
prerequisites: A hypothesis-driven experiment written to the JEI template (life + experimental physical sciences).
costNote: $49 submission fee (the only fee); need-based waivers available.
howToStart: Run a hypothesis-driven experiment, write to the template, submit via Editorial Manager (or request a waiver).
a2cInsight: The legit, near-free, genuinely peer-reviewed science journal — meaningful precisely because review is real. NOT pay-to-publish.

name: Frontiers for Young Minds (Young Reviewer)
ageNote: Ages 8-15 peer-REVIEW real scientific manuscripts (a Science Mentor pairs with them).
prerequisites: Curiosity; the published review is signed — real reviewer credit on a real journal.
howToStart: Sign up as a Young Reviewer at kids.frontiersin.org/participate/young-minds.
a2cInsight: Uniquely accessible AND genuinely prestigious for the 8-15 band — a signed reviewer credit on a real journal.

name: The Concord Review
ageNote: Grades 9-12; the word-count/scholarship bar excludes most middle schoolers.
prerequisites: An original, heavily-endnoted ~5,000-8,000+ word history research essay (Chicago/Turabian).
howToStart: Read past issues to calibrate, write a Chicago-endnoted history essay, submit at tcr.org/submit.
a2cInsight: The gold standard for HS humanities — the rare journal admissions officers actually respect. Top essays win the Emerson Prize.

name: The Adroit Journal (Adroit Prizes)
ageNote: Adroit Prizes for secondary + undergrad (~ages 13-19); the teen on-ramp.
prerequisites: Polished original poetry, fiction, or creative nonfiction with a distinctive voice.
cadence: Adroit Prizes ~Apr-mid May annually; ~1-2% general acceptance.
howToStart: Enter the Adroit Prizes via Submittable in spring; use the fee-waiver if needed.
a2cInsight: A gold-standard teen literary credential alongside Scholastic; a win/finalist is a true needle-mover.

name: Blue Marble Review
ageNote: Ages 13-22 — accepts middle schoolers.
prerequisites: Original writing or art; curated, ~22% acceptance.
costNote: Free — and PAYS contributors $30/piece (a legitimacy signal).
howToStart: Submit via the on-site form; use a personal email so acceptance replies aren't filtered.
a2cInsight: A clean early/first byline; strong entry point because it accepts younger writers and pays.

name: Literature review (self-directed)
ageNote: The single most accessible genuine FIRST paper — no lab or data collection.
prerequisites: A narrow question + 15-30 peer-reviewed sources; synthesis (surface debates/gaps), not summary.
howToStart: Pick a narrow question, read 15-30 papers, synthesize; submit to a legit student journal or post on Zenodo.
a2cInsight: T3 self-posted, T2 in a legit student journal — the most accessible legitimate route to a real first paper.

name: GitHub project write-up / technical report
ageNote: In CS/ML, arguably the most respected self-directed output — reviewers can run it.
prerequisites: A working project; write the README AS a paper (problem -> method -> data -> results -> reproduce).
howToStart: Build it, document it as a paper, tag a release + Zenodo DOI; credibility = reproducibility.
a2cInsight: High-functioning T3 — for a builder-track CS student, a reproducible repo beats a pay-to-publish journal.

name: Kaggle Competitions (Grandmaster path)
ageNote: 13+ to participate (minors need verifiable parental consent); medals/rank NOT prize-gated.
prerequisites: Python + pandas/scikit-learn; for top results: gradient boosting, PyTorch, rigorous cross-validation.
costNote: Free (free GPU/TPU notebooks); cash prizes generally require 18+.
cadence: Featured comps have deadlines; Getting Started comps never close.
howToStart: Account (parental consent if under 18) -> Titanic -> fork a starter notebook -> monthly Playground Series.
a2cInsight: Genuinely prestigious AND attainable by a consenting 13+ teen. Trust your own cross-validation, not the public leaderboard.

name: AMC 8
ageNote: Grade 8 and below; the entry rung of the AMC ladder.
prerequisites: Middle-school math + problem-solving practice (AoPS/Alcumus).
costNote: Small per-student fee, usually paid by the host school.
cadence: Annually in January; register through a school or a registered site in the fall.
howToStart: Find a hosting school/center; practice past papers; the first rung of AMC 8 -> AMC 10 -> AIME.
a2cInsight: A great first win and the on-ramp to the most recognized math ladder. Participation is T3; advancement is the goal.

name: MATHCOUNTS Competition Series
ageNote: Grades 6-8 — the flagship middle-school math competition.
prerequisites: A school team/coach; sustained problem-solving practice.
cadence: School -> Chapter -> State -> National (Feb-May); register in the FALL.
howToStart: Join or start a school MATHCOUNTS team in the fall; practice with past rounds.
a2cInsight: The premier MS math signal; advancing to State/Nationals is a genuine T1-leaning distinction.

name: AMC 10
ageNote: Grade 10 and below — strong middle schoolers take it early.
prerequisites: Algebra + geometry fluency; AMC 8 background helps.
cadence: November; register in the fall. Qualifies for AIME.
howToStart: Register through a school/site in the fall; a high score qualifies you for the AIME.
a2cInsight: The gateway to AIME -> USA(J)MO. A qualifying score is a real differentiator.

name: AIME (American Invitational Mathematics Examination)
ageNote: Invitation-only via AMC 10/12; exceptional middle schoolers reach it.
prerequisites: Qualify via AMC 10/12.
howToStart: Qualify through the AMC 10/12; the rung below USA(J)MO.
a2cInsight: A national-flagship math signal; reaching AIME is a recognized scarcity marker.

name: 3M Young Scientist Challenge
ageNote: Grades 5-8 — a premier middle-school science competition.
prerequisites: A 1-2 minute video pitching a solution to an everyday problem.
cadence: Entries spring; finalists summer; finals fall.
howToStart: Submit a short video describing an innovation; 10 finalists get a summer mentorship.
a2cInsight: A genuine T1 MS science signal with a mentorship prize; one of the best early science showcases.

name: Thermo Fisher Junior Innovators Challenge (JIC)
ageNote: Grades 6-8 — formerly Broadcom MASTERS; the top MS science-fair capstone.
prerequisites: Nomination from a partner regional/state science fair (top ~10%).
costNote: Free; reached via a feeder science fair.
howToStart: Do a science-fair project, place at an affiliated regional fair, get nominated.
a2cInsight: The premier middle-school research distinction and the rung below ISEF on the science ladder.

name: Scholastic Art & Writing Awards
ageNote: Grades 7-12 — the most prestigious creative-teen program; MS welcome.
prerequisites: Original art or writing in one of 28+ categories.
costNote: Per-submission fee with fee waivers available.
cadence: Regional submissions ~Sept-Dec; National Medals in spring.
howToStart: Submit work in the fall through the regional affiliate; a Gold Key climbs toward a National Medal.
a2cInsight: Filed as a competition; the National Medal is a T1 award. Regional Gold Key -> National Medal is the creative ladder.

name: USACO (USA Computing Olympiad)
ageNote: No age floor; strong MS coders start at Bronze.
prerequisites: Programming (C++/Java/Python) + algorithms; self-study friendly.
cadence: Four contest weekends Dec-Feb; promotion within a contest.
howToStart: Make an account, start Bronze with the USACO Guide; climb Bronze -> Silver -> Gold -> Platinum.
a2cInsight: Bronze is Intro difficulty but the USACO brand is T1 — a dated, ungameable algorithm-track ladder.

name: picoCTF (Carnegie Mellon)
ageNote: Beginner-friendly capture-the-flag; great MS cybersecurity on-ramp.
prerequisites: Curiosity; basic CS helps. Challenges teach as you go.
howToStart: Make an account and work the picoGym; enter the annual picoCTF event.
a2cInsight: A friendly first rung on the security track (Bebras/picoCTF -> USACO).

name: Congressional App Challenge (CAC)
ageNote: Middle + high school; one winner PER congressional district -> tiny pool.
prerequisites: An original app in any language; solo or small team.
cadence: Register summer; submit by early November.
howToStart: Build an app, record a demo video, submit before the fall deadline.
a2cInsight: Intro/T3 but reads above its weight — district-level and Congress-backed. A great builder-track first win.

name: Verizon Innovative Learning App Challenge
ageNote: Middle + high school teams; concept-stage (no code required).
prerequisites: A team + a teacher sponsor; an app concept addressing a community need.
howToStart: Form a team with a teacher sponsor, design an app concept, submit in the fall.
a2cInsight: The gentlest builder-track on-ramp (Verizon -> Congressional App Challenge -> hackathons -> a deployed app).

name: Science Olympiad (Division B)
ageNote: Division B = grades 6-9; team-based across 20+ events.
prerequisites: A school team; preparation in chosen events.
cadence: Regional -> State -> National (winter-spring); join the team in the fall.
howToStart: Join your school's Science Olympiad team and pick events that fit your interests.
a2cInsight: A strong, broad MS STEM team experience; State/National medals are real distinctions.

name: FIRST LEGO League (FLL)
ageNote: Ages 9-16 — the MS entry to the FIRST robotics ladder (FLL -> FTC -> FRC).
prerequisites: A team + coach; build/program a LEGO robot + a research project.
costNote: Team + kit costs; grants often available.
cadence: Season runs fall-winter; register in late summer/fall.
howToStart: Join or start an FLL team in the fall; compete in the regional tournament.
a2cInsight: The foundational rung of the FIRST robotics ladder; sustained involvement builds toward FTC/FRC leadership.

name: Technovation Girls
ageNote: Ages 8-18 girls/non-binary; build an app + a business plan for a community problem.
prerequisites: A team + mentor; an app idea solving a real-world problem.
howToStart: Form a team with a mentor, pick a community problem, build and pitch the app.
a2cInsight: A genuine intersection of CS + entrepreneurship + impact; a regional/global placement is a strong T2 signal.

name: National History Day (NHD)
ageNote: Junior division = grades 6-8; the on-ramp to the history-writing ladder.
prerequisites: A researched paper, exhibit, documentary, performance, or website on an annual theme.
howToStart: Pick a topic on the year's theme, research it deeply, enter at the school/regional level.
a2cInsight: NHD Junior -> World Historian Essay -> The Concord Review is the history-writing ladder.

name: Math Kangaroo USA
ageNote: Grades 1-12; a low-stakes, fun problem-solving contest.
prerequisites: None beyond grade-level math curiosity.
costNote: ~$21 registration; register in the fall/early winter.
cadence: Annually in March; register by mid-December.
howToStart: Register by December at mathkangaroo.org; great early habit-builder.
a2cInsight: A confidence-building early contest; momentum, not a resume centerpiece.

name: eCYBERMISSION (US Army / AEOP)
ageNote: Grades 6-9; team STEM project solving a community problem.
prerequisites: A team of 3-4 + a Team Advisor.
costNote: Free; cash awards as savings bonds.
howToStart: Form a team with an advisor, identify a local problem, run the project, submit.
a2cInsight: A free, accessible MS team-science on-ramp with real community framing.

name: Bow Seat Ocean Awareness Contest
ageNote: Ages 11-18 — art/writing/film on ocean & climate themes.
prerequisites: A creative piece (visual art, poetry, prose, film, music) on the annual theme.
howToStart: Make a creative piece on the year's ocean-awareness theme and submit online.
a2cInsight: A clean intersection of art/writing + environment for the creatively-inclined kid.

name: Regeneron ISEF (International Science & Engineering Fair)
ageNote: Grades 9-12; the world's top pre-college science fair (the north star).
prerequisites: Win/place at an affiliated regional or state fair to qualify.
costNote: Free; reached via a feeder fair.
cadence: Affiliated fairs winter-spring; ISEF in May.
howToStart: Do original research, place at an affiliated fair, advance to ISEF.
a2cInsight: An ISEF finalist is among the highest-signal Tier-1 STEM outcomes. The science-research north star.

name: Regeneron Science Talent Search (STS)
ageNote: Seniors only — the summit of the US science-research ladder.
prerequisites: An original, sustained research project + full application as a HS senior.
cadence: Application in fall of senior year.
howToStart: Build a multi-year research project; apply senior fall. (Start the arc now.)
a2cInsight: The most prestigious US pre-college science award; the multi-year arc that early starters can uniquely build.

name: Davidson Young Scholars (DYS)
ageNote: Ages 5-18 (apply by ~16); free support for profoundly gifted students.
prerequisites: Evidence of profound giftedness (qualifying test scores / portfolio).
howToStart: Apply with qualifying evidence at davidsongifted.org/young-scholars.
a2cInsight: A free, selective recognition + support network for highly gifted young students.

name: Johns Hopkins CTY — Talent Search
ageNote: Grades 2-8 take above-grade tests for recognition + course eligibility.
prerequisites: An above-grade-level qualifying test (e.g., SCAT).
costNote: Test + program fees; financial aid available.
howToStart: Register for the qualifying test; high scores earn recognition + CTY course access.
a2cInsight: A recognized early talent-search credential and a gateway to acceleration.

name: National Latin Exam (NLE)
ageNote: Any Latin student; a stackable, low-stakes language honor.
prerequisites: Enrollment in (or self-study of) Latin at the exam level.
cadence: Annually in spring; register through a school in winter.
howToStart: Take Latin (or self-study), register through a school, sit the exam for a medal/certificate.
a2cInsight: A clean, repeatable recognition that compounds year over year for a languages-classics student.

name: Doodle for Google
ageNote: Grades K-12; a national art contest on an annual theme.
prerequisites: An original doodle on the year's theme + a short artist statement.
howToStart: Create a themed doodle and submit with a parent/guardian sign-off.
a2cInsight: A fun, accessible national art recognition; a confidence-builder for young visual artists.

name: National Junior Honor Society (NJHS)
ageNote: Grades 6-9; school-chapter membership on scholarship + service + leadership.
prerequisites: GPA threshold + demonstrated service/leadership/character.
howToStart: Meet your school chapter's criteria and apply when invited.
a2cInsight: Membership alone is T3 filler; pursue a leadership role to make it count.

name: The Congressional Award
ageNote: Ages 13.5-24; self-set goals across service, fitness, and exploration.
prerequisites: Register and log progress toward self-set goals (Bronze -> Gold).
howToStart: Register, set goals, and log hours toward the Bronze medal first.
a2cInsight: A stackable official recognition; pair it with a sustained, measurable project (the spike itself).

name: Conrad Challenge
ageNote: Ages 13-18 teams; innovation + entrepreneurship to solve global problems.
prerequisites: A team + coach; a product/venture concept with a business plan.
costNote: Free to enter early rounds; finals travel costs vary.
howToStart: Form a team, pick a challenge category, build the concept + plan, submit each round.
a2cInsight: A strong STEM + entrepreneurship intersection for team-oriented builders.

name: Zenodo (DOI repository)
ageNote: Any age can upload; useful with a real dataset/codebase/poster.
prerequisites: A report, dataset, poster, or code release worth archiving.
howToStart: Upload + metadata + license; for code, link a GitHub Release to auto-mint a DOI.
a2cInsight: The standard 'give my code/data a DOI' move. A DOI makes work citable — it is NOT peer review or prestige.

name: Curieux Academic Journal
ageNote: Middle + high school; accepts middle schoolers.
prerequisites: A manuscript; markets a two-editor review but high acceptance (~60-70% est.).
costNote: Free to submit; on acceptance ~$185-250 (fast-track upsell).
howToStart: Fine only as an early publishing EXPERIENCE for a younger student — never present it as a credential.
a2cInsight: Textbook pay-to-publish / accept-almost-everyone; the fast-track upsell is exactly what ProPublica flagged. Adds little admissions value.

name: International Journal of High School Research (IJHSR)
ageNote: HS (global); publisher is Terra Science. Do NOT confuse with the unrelated ijhsr.org.
prerequisites: A science manuscript; controversially requires the student to SUPPLY three reviewers.
howToStart: Treat skeptically — never present as a credential.
a2cInsight: Named by ProPublica; effectively pay-to-publish, and author-supplied reviewers offload peer review onto the student's contacts.

name: Journal of Student Research (JSR) — HS Edition
ageNote: HS edition; widely marketed to teens.
prerequisites: A manuscript; pay-to-publish pattern with a fast-track upsell.
costNote: Fee on acceptance; expedited review upsell.
howToStart: Avoid as a credential; a legit student journal or a preprint is a better route.
a2cInsight: Named by ProPublica — fee-on-acceptance + fast-track upsell. The confusingly-similar name is itself a red flag.

name: Questioz — International Journal for HS Research
ageNote: HS with an advisor (required).
prerequisites: A research manuscript + advisor; student-run review (~4 wk).
howToStart: Submit at questioz.org; useful as experience, not as a distinction.
a2cInsight: Legit-ish and free, but high acceptance reads more as participation than achievement.

name: President's Volunteer Service Award (PVSA)
ageNote: Any age; log service hours for an automatic federal medal.
prerequisites: Logged volunteer hours through a certifying organization.
costNote: Small certification fee via the certifying org.
howToStart: Verify relaunch before recommending; historically log hours via a certifying organization.
a2cInsight: PVSA alone is Tier-4 filler — a supporting line, not the spike. Paused as of 2026.

name: Junior Science & Humanities Symposium (JSHS)
ageNote: HS research presentation program; DoD-sponsored.
prerequisites: An original research project to present.
cadence: On hiatus — confirm relaunch before recommending.
howToStart: Verify status before recommending; historically present research at the regional symposium.
a2cInsight: Historically a respected research-presentation venue; suspended as of 2026 — watch for relaunch.

name: Duke TIP (Talent Identification Program)
ageNote: Former gifted talent search.
prerequisites: n/a — program ended.
howToStart: Do not pursue — Duke TIP has permanently ended; consider CTY or Belin-Blank instead.
a2cInsight: Listed only so families don't chase a dead program. Redirect to active talent searches.

name: National Geographic GeoBee
ageNote: Former grades 4-8 geography bee.
prerequisites: n/a — discontinued.
howToStart: Do not pursue — discontinued; consider US Geography Championships / IAC Geography Olympiad instead.
a2cInsight: Listed only to steer families off a dead program. Redirect to active geography contests.
